title: Lesson 12 — Moods

words.example: He sat morose in the corner, saying nothing all night.

words.definition: a feeling of pensive, lingering sadness
words.example: A melancholy tune drifted from the old piano.
A sullen teenager slammed the door and said nothing.
The dog gave a doleful whine at the closed door.
Ebullient fans spilled into the streets after the win.
An effervescent host who lit up the whole room.

words.definition: optimistic or positive, especially in a difficult situation
words.example: She stayed sanguine despite the string of setbacks.

words.definition: calmness and composure, especially under strain
words.example: He accepted the harsh verdict with surprising equanimity.
A phlegmatic goalkeeper, unrattled by the roaring crowd.
A petulant complaint about the seating arrangement.

words.definition: complaining in a petulant or whining manner
words.example: A querulous guest who found fault with every dish.
A sardonic smile was his only reply to the bad news.

story: Backstage, the cast's moods clashed. The lead was [[morose|morose]] and full of [[melancholy|melancholy]]; the understudy plain [[sullen|sullen]]; and the stagehand wore a [[doleful|doleful]] look all evening. Against them stood the [[ebullient|ebullient]], [[effervescent|effervescent]] director, [[sanguine|sanguine]] that the night would go well. The producer met every crisis with unshakable [[equanimity|equanimity]], as [[phlegmatic|phlegmatic]] as a stone. Only the diva spoiled the calm, [[petulant|petulant]] about her dressing room and [[querulous|querulous]] over the lights, until the [[sardonic|sardonic]] old prompter muttered that the real drama was always offstage.
